input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>

+  </ItemGroup>

+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet doesn't recognize `net10.0` as a runtime yet. I'll run the benchmarks separately for each framework and compare.
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet doesn't support .NET 10 as a named runtime yet. I need a different approach. Let me use a custom job with a manually-specified runtime, or I can just run the benchmarks separately — once targeting net9.0 and once targeting net10.0 — and compare the results. Actually, the simplest approach is to use the `--runtimes net90` (which is a BDN alias). For .NET 10, since BDN doesn't have it, I can:

1. Run benchmarks on net9.0 TFM, then on net10.0 TFM separately


2. Or create a custom runtime

I think the cleanest solution is to just run the benchmarks twice separately — once with `--framework net9.0` and again with `--framework net10.0` — using the default job configuration each time.
